speaker: interviewee
text: [BEEP]

speaker: interviewer
text: [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, it's the 18th of June, 1995. The survival is John Horak. The interviewer is Gretta Rusanow. We are in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia, and we're speaking in English. And go.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's June 18, 1995. I am Gretta Rusanow, G-R-E-T-T-A R-U-S-A-N-O-W. Abd I'm interviewing John Horak today in Sydney, Australia, and the interview will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, the camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: John Horak.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-O-R-A-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: At birth, my name was Eugen Jan Hirschmann.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-I-R-S-C-H-M-A-double N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you ever have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had-- in my illegal life, I had three other names.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me what they are just at the moment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hanak, Hamaj, and [? Mehorek ?] sometimes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you spell those for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: H-A-- the first one, Hanak, H-A-N-A-K. Hamaj, H-A-M-A-J.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: My birth date is 29th of December, 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old does that make you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 75, unfortunately.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] And what city and country where you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Trenc Tepla. That was a small country town in Czechoslovakia-- actually, in the Slovakian part because Czechoslovakia had three different nationalities, Bohemia, Moravia, and Slovakia. And we were the eastern part of Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe Trenc Tepla for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trenc Tepla was a small country town, or village, maybe. But it was very important because it was very near to a thermal place in Slovakia, which is a mountainous country. We were fortunate we had a lot of thermal water in different places of the country. And three of the most important one was one place which was called Trenc Teplice. And the village where I was born, Trenc Tepla, part of the same name, was about four and a half kilometers. And we had the railway station because Trenc Teplice, the thermal place, was up on a mountain range, and the train couldn't possibly go so far. So our main station was in my village of [? Burs ?] where all the trains came. And from there we had a [NON-ENGLISH], a street car railway, which took the guests, mainly overseas visitors, that took the two-- four and a half to five kilometers to the thermal place, Trenc Teplice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the population of Trenc Tepla?

speaker: interviewee
text: Trenc Tepla population could have been in normal times about 2,000 people only. But in the time of the season, which was about six months during the year, the place grew to double the size because they came lot of people working in the thermal place, and they were not living in the-- in the holiday resort because it was small and very expensive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were living in our village and went daily to their workplace. So maybe we had in this seasons sometimes 4,000, sometimes four and a half thousand people. And what was the Jewish population-- the size of the Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish population in my little [? Burs ?] town could have been-- I can only guess-- about 20, 25 families. So-- so the population was possibly 250 people-- souls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me about your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family-- then my father was a businessman, like, he was the owner of a country store, a general country store.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel Hirschmann. He established that shop of his, of his firm, before the first World War. And must have been very diligent and able because he build it up to a very prosperous business. The-- the place where-- where we had-- there we are living and we had the business was also important for the whole district because we had the sugar refinery there. The whole area, or the district of-- of Tepla or Teplice, was a sugar-growing district, and the-- the sugar refinery was established there by German Jewish people, which I found out later on. They must have been establishing this sugar refinery maybe about 30 to 40 years before the first World War. So it was a well-established and well-known place. And that was the reason why my father most probably decided to have a-- a, like, a general store, not a small grocery shop. He became more and more known in the whole area because he was distributor of the sugar, which he got directly from the sugar refinery, and also distributor for smaller shops in the area, for smaller grocery shops who couldn't afford to buy in bulk. My father used to buy everything in bulk. That was his strength.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was unfortunately enslaved in the business of the family, as it was usually the way, especially because we were four children. So to bring up four children was not easy. And my father, because of his ambition and ability to be a successful businessman, he was constantly traveling-- I mean, at least once in a week. One day in the week, he was either in Bratislava, which was the capital town of Slovakia, or he traveled even to Moravia to purchase lot of items which were not available in his own district.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your mother-- she worked in the business as well as raising--

speaker: interviewee
text: She worked in the business because you know how it is, a country store in the beginning of 1900 was built mainly on the family ambition and diligence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gizella.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gizella who?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hirschmann. Her maiden name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Ungar. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me about your brothers and-- and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were four children, as I mentioned. My oldest brother was six years older from me. The next one in line was my sister. She was four years older than me. Then my third brother was two-- always two years in between, which was a very [LAUGHS] economical arrangement.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you all work in the family business as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: We worked only when we were teenagers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Like, on a country store, usually a family of four or six, all the children had to help. We-- we went to schools, of course. But in the afternoon, we had to help. But mind you, we had lot of people working for us. I-- in my teenage years, I can remember we had about six to eight people working, paid employees. So it was a-- quite a good business.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about school in Trenc Tepla.

speaker: interviewee
text: I started school and I was five and a half or six years of age. We had only government schools. There were no private schools. And also something which I can mention that we had-- I was born in the middle of Slovakia, which was, like, middle geographically. And that was a strictly Slovakian area because Slovakia was bordered with Hungary, with Poland, and with Moravia. And each border line was influenced by the different languages of these parts. Like, for instance, the Jewish people who were living close to the Hungarian border, their mother language, more or less, was Hungarian. We were born-- or my family was in the middle of Slovakia, and we-- my mother language is Slovak. We spoke Slovakian at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What other languages did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: German I understood, but as a small child, I didn't speak German or Hungarian. But you get used to the-- your ear gets used to it. But my parents were very advanced, I could say, because my mother couldn't manage the business and to look after the children. So they-- she engaged always a nanny for the children, which was a local girl. But when we were already in the years of four or six, then we had a [GERMAN]. That is a-- a-- a children nanny, or how would you say that. And she usually came from the German part of Czechoslovakia. The German part of Czechoslovakia was up north at the Bohemia-- Sudetenland, which people--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --would have a idea of where it was. And they engaged-- my parents wanted then the children should learn as many languages as possible, all the languages which were important for our country. So that was first of all German. Hungarian as a second useful language.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had a housekeeper, usually either a Slovakian woman who did the rough work, or Hungarian. So we as children heard-- my parents spoke the three languages fluently. So we as children got used to the different languages and learned it the easy way. So as a child what languages did you speak?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- I started to speak a little bit of German when I entered the primary school through the constant company of my-- my-- how would you say it-- [GERMAN] is a children's maid. She was looking only after us children-- nothing of the housework. She was like a family member.

speaker: interviewer
text: And tell me about Jewish life in your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were leading a Jewish house, kosher household, of course.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hmm.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a kosher butcher in the village, which is also unusual. Usually, the small places didn't have a kosher butcher. But the 30 families which were living in that village. And mainly in the six months during the year, our village became very important because of the influx of the foreign tourists. And business-wise, it was very, very useful and profitable. So we had many shops, which normally a place of 2,000 people would have, let's say, three or four. We had about eight to twelve grocery shops and-- and food shops. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: As well as the general store? Or-- or you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, all together.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: A general store--

speaker: interviewer
text: In the town, you mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: In the town, in the village of Trenc Tepla, the place where I was born. When you say there were no general stores in the sense what we used to know now, but they were-- there were shops which made quite lot of turnover during the season, during the thermal place season.

speaker: interviewer
text: So people who came there as guests from lot of European countries. Even I remember English people came quite a lot because the thermal water was 80 degrees Celsius hot. It was really helping for rheumatic people, suffering from rheumatism. There were two or three other places, but Trenc Teplice-- I would say the second in the country. And how long did your family stay in Trenc Tepla for?

speaker: interviewee
text: The family-- as-- as a family, we were staying in Trenc Tepla till 1930 or '31 because my father was very ambitious and because of traveling so often to the capital town of Slovakia, where he purchased from the importers, we used to sell-- I have to mention it-- we used to sell, for instance, dates, bananas, oranges. And this was in the beginning of the 1920 and '25-- very unusual. But he knew very well, as a-- as a very ambitious business man, that this delicatessens which he sold are very important part of his business because the people used to come from the nearby thermal place and bought these things, or he delivered it to some of the smaller shops in the thermal place itself. So he was very ambitious, and he went and bought things which the other shops didn't dare to buy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he-- why did he move to Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because he was financially so well off and his observing of the more [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] better life in the big cities. Like, Bratislava was the capital town, and there were-- in Bratislava, there were in normal times-- when I say 'normal' times, in the older times-- there were 30,000 or 35,000 Jewish people from 140,000. And all-- lot of his friends there in the capital town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Matter of fact, one of his youngest brother, who was a very good dentist, had his dentistry in Bratislava. So went quite often to see him. And maybe he was a little bit over-ambitious and jealous, and he-- his brother lives in the big town. So he started to speculate, saying, how can we all come and live in Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Such opportunity came along when one of his business friends, a agent of business, suggested to him because he knew that we were financially well off. And he persuaded my father to come to Bratislava to establish a completely different business and be part of the big, good life in the capital town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember moving to Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because I-- I just finished the fifth class of primary school. The primary school was five years, right? And I entered the-- the school in 1925. And when I finished the first five years of the primary school, then-- then my father arranged. My mother didn't want to go. She resisted, actually, over a year. But in the end, she has to give in because my father sold the-- the house, sold the business, which was a gold mine in the little village, and moved to Bratislava and started completely different business-- a fashion business, wholesale, not retail.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how old were you when you moved to Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I moved to Bratislava, I could have been 10 [INAUDIBLE] 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe for me-- can you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what your impressions were of this big town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- the children, specially the younger ones-- were not very happy because, you know, the good life which we had from the-- on the country town was-- was very satisfying. We had-- we were the number one family, maybe, there. I had-- I remember then I had the first soccer ball. And in 1925, to have a soccer ball was already a sensation. So-- and we had a huge property, because we had a-- a huge garden behind the-- the house, and a big yard, because we sold also building materials and all the other things which normally the other shops didn't have. So we used to have the biggest yard and the biggest garden in town. And whenever I wanted to have a-- a soccer match, so I got the whole village. The children would have come from everywhere just to play with a real, proper soccer ball. So it was a satisfying life. And of course, when we had to move, because after all, as a child we didn't have any authoration or possibility, so we moved to the big town. I remember then I wasn't very happy because it was completely different lifestyle. You know, the-- the people in every major city consider country people like a country people, you know, that's not very respectable. But I settled. I had to settle.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I continued my-- my first class of [NON-ENGLISH], which is the high school. In European countries the high school is called [NON-ENGLISH], all right? So I went there. I was enrolled in the [NON-ENGLISH], which was a very big change because the children of the-- of the big city-- were all so different. It wasn't the country type of a life.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I settled quite well. And after having-- with almost-- I finished four years of the high school. But I suddenly changed my view what I want to be. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what-- what did you think you wanted to be?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, a-- a child of-- of a Jewish family, a prosperous Jewish family, they always wanted even that time to study for a lawyer or a doctor and so on. That-- this didn't change. But I didn't like-- when it came to the third year of the high school, we started to learn Latin. And Latin was a very difficult language, and I didn't like it very much. So naturally, I wasn't keen to learn something which I didn't like. And there-- it was a quite a hard study.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was under the influence of two very good friends of mine in the same class, and those two youngsters were very gifted comics. Like all school children, they developed such liking. And those two friends of mine, they said, oh, they didn't like Latin right away.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, we will-- next year, we are going to enroll in the dramatic art academy to be a actor. And it appealed to me [LAUGHS] was probably because I didn't say anything at home. Secretly, I enrolled also in the dramatic art. That was halfway of your eight-year study of [NON-ENGLISH]-- it was eight years.

speaker: interviewer
text: When my parents found out, then-- what he wants to be a actor? They were terribly annoyed because who wants-- actor was not a very respectable profession. It was more or less considered like a gypsy. And gypsies and actors were not the favorite of the people anywhere in Europe at that time. So my father was very angry, and without telling me what he wanted to do, he, through his connections in the-- in the new business, which he had already for some time, in the ladies' and mens' fashion-- That was his new business?

speaker: interviewee
text: New business, yes. And he-- he had lot of friends who-- who were manufacturer, especially in the other part of Czechoslovakia because Moravia was the nearest country. Moravians are Czech, speaking Czech. Czech and Slovak, for instance-- the two languages are completely different. If I-- I remember the the first teachers or professors in my high school. They were mostly Czechs, because Slovakia was under 1,000 years of occupation of Hungarian, Austro-Hungarian empire. Bohemia and Moravia was under the influence of the culture influence of Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my professors were mainly Czech because the Slovaks didn't have a-- a crop of teachers. It-- it has got significance later on in my life, which I didn't realize as a child. And this influence of-- of Czech teacher taught me maybe in a better quality teachings than the Slovak teachers would have because they had to learn themselves first.

speaker: interviewee
text: And under such circumstances, I-- I was quite happy in any school. If I went to the [NON-ENGLISH] [INAUDIBLE] if I wanted to be in the-- in the dramatic art, it would have changed my life, of course. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do?

speaker: interviewee
text: So my father got in touch, without telling me, of course-- because in that time, a child of 14 or 15 was considered as a child. And you would not ask what you want to do. You were told what to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he arranged through his connections in the other part of Czechoslovakia with a manufacturer who was a big manufacturer for textiles. He said, if he can get me in the-- there was a famous textile academy in that town. It was about 220, 250 kilometers away from-- from Bratislava. If we talk about 200 kilometers, it's different like here because it's a huge distance, right? You travel four to five hours by-- by a fast train. So he went there. Before the school year started, the last week before a new school year, he packed a suitcase-- or my mother and father-- and they-- he took me on the train and brought me to this town of Brno, which was the seat of a much larger town that was the center of the textile industry in the middle of Czechoslovakia. And he-- he placed me in care of his friends, of this friend who arranged the enrollment in the textile academy in Brno. I must mention, maybe, that the textile academy in Brno was one of the best in Europe. There was only other one which was equal-- that's what they said-- in Manchester, in England. Because Czechoslovakia, the-- the second largest industry was textiles, all right? And that was the-- the-- Brno was the-- the-- the center of the textile-- wool and cotton-- industry. So when I was brought there, like in exile, of course, you can imagine I wasn't very happy. The friend of my father enrolled me in the textile academy because it was very difficult to get in.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only with connections-- only the children of textile manufacturers and-- and similar had the-- the ability to go there normally, because there were maybe 25 to 30 students per class, and there were only two classes to begin with. So they-- the overseas-- they-- half of them were usually from other countries, overseas visitors, because the textile industry was very important all over Europe. So you had to have good connections to get a child to this sort of academy. But what they didn't know, or-- or didn't want to know, when-- when my-- the friend of my father enrolled me in the textile academy, there were two, because they-- the school was established 1740 or '50. And it was originally a German school. But when Czechoslovakia was established-- that was in 1918-- the state arranged and they started was the textile academy in Czech. That was the language of the country. But the better school was the historical one, which was a German. And naturally, a Jewish friend of my father-- they spoke German at home. He was-- you see, many Jewish people in our country were either German-minded or Hungarian, or something in between which developed only later as Czechoslovakian. And I belonged culturally to the Czechoslovakian-minded group.

speaker: interviewer
text: But he was German-minded.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was German-minded. My name was originally Hirschmann, which is a typical German name. So naturally, he enrolled me in a German school. And when I arrived there, that was the first shock, the first surprise, because I understood German, but I spoke very, very little of it. We spoke at home Slovak. Even now, with my dear wife, I speak Slovak because that is my mother language. And that was the shock for me because when I entered the classroom and I faced professors-- and the German section of the textile academy was on a very high standard. There were hardly any books to-- because for instance, we had professor who was engineer, doctor, and I don't know what else-- two titles at least. And they start-- they entered the class. We had hardly any books. And he started to dictate, and our-- our instructions were, you make notes. And that's it. We had few books of the basic knowledge about textile industry.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was lost, absolutely. So much so that I was terribly unhappy and didn't know what to do. I couldn't just pack up and go home. I was still a youngster. The distance was great, so-- How old were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 13, going on 14-- 13 and 1/2, something like that. That was the-- in 1936. No, 16 because '36. So I was terribly unhappy and I didn't know what to. And I was a good student back home, and here I was just out of the race.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in desperation, I-- I had the courage-- because it took courage-- to see the headmaster of the whole school. It was Mr. [? Baier. ?] I remember, a German. Because we had lot of German, the ethnic Germans-- Sudetendeutsche and ethnic Germans-- and they were almost everywhere. So I approached the-- the headmaster with lot of courage and-- and fright. I said, look, this is my problem. I can't-- I can't go back. So he said, yes, that is a problem. And he was maybe feeling sorry for me. He said, I-- let me about it. I'll let you know what we can [LAUGHS] do with you. So about in the daytime, he-- he called me, I think, the next day. He said, you know, I spoke to two of the main professors, like, one was teaching the technology of weaving, the other one, a chemistry of textiles, which are very-- two important subjects.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said, you know, I arranged without asking you because I think that is the best for you. You will write in whichever way you want-- half Slovak, half German, your notes. They will not be classified. And-- and the answers you give in your best mastered German, what you can do. And that will go on for six months. You will not be classified like a normal student.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't have any chance whatsoever to say yes or no. I had to be happy with that. But to me, it was-- I was terribly angry and unhappy. And maybe that gave me extra courage to prove to myself and to my parents, and I am not-- and I didn't go home for half a year, because you had the holidays every two or three months. I was hurt. I didn't go home.

speaker: interviewee
text: And really, I did my best, and I did it with utmost difficulty. But slowly and slowly I started to speak better German. I wrote maybe very bad German because German wasn't easy for me. But they couldn't care less if is this grammatically right, my notes, as long--. And also for my schoolwork, because we had exams. They said, all right, Horak will write whichever way. They were able to get the meaning, and I was classified only after six months. So I was doing not well, but quite all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you finish that course?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished. I was there two years.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year did you finish?

speaker: interviewee
text: I finished in '38. I went '36, '37, '38 school year, and then Germany occupied our country. That--

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was beginning of '39. In March '39, the country was actually completely destroyed by German occupation. Germany occupied Bohemia and Moravia, and Slovakia, by mysterious way, became a independent, small republic.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did those events affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much, very much so. That actually changed my whole life. You see, not many people even now knew then the so-called independent Republic of Slovakia, which was only a small part of the original Czechoslovakian Republic. And Hitler decided in 1939, in March '39, then Slovakia will become a independent Slovakian Republic. Nobody knew. I actually found out because politics is my-- my hobby. Then why did a mighty empire-- because Germany was really the strongest country in '38, '39 in Europe. And why would they give independence to a small Slovakian people, which were-- 2.5 million were Slovaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you thought at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not at the time. I thought doing the war many times. And during the war, I had also few indications why it happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: But at that time, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't. Nobody knew. Not only me. I was just a youngster. Nobody knew the real reason why the big, mighty Germany occupied absolutely with power and strength Bohemia and Moravia, which is two sorts of Czechoslovakia, and they were called [GERMAN]. And Jews were taken immediately. The Jewish population of these two countries were 8,000 in Moravia-- in Bohemia, about 5,000 in Moravia. About 12 to 13 million people were from the population. And Jews were about, I would say-- 80,000, 40-- about 140,000 to 150,000 Jews were living in the two part of Czechoslovakia which was completely occupied by Germany. Slovakia had about 60,000, 75,000 Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you finished your course, and then Hitler invaded.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that--- how did that impact on your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't finish the-- because I was-- I was ready to go for four years because that was the four years course. That was on the-- on a standard of a technical college of a-- like university with the title of engineer and so on. And I was ready because I was starting to do all right, and I was proud what I was doing. I was sure that I will be there till I finish my study of four years. But didn't happen because the political changes came. When-- when Hitler occupied Bohemia and Moravia militaristically, so they-- they considered me as a Slovakian citizen, coming from Slovakia. That was a different country. German army didn't occupy Slovakia. We became independent.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they declared one day in the school, every foreign nationals must go home. And I was-- maybe that was lucky because that saved my life in the long run. So I was forced to go home to Slovakia, and there I was. And the anti-Jewish paragraphs, the Nuremberg laws, they were introduced immediately in Bohemia and Moravia very strictly. All Czech Jews were already sent to-- to concentration camps in Theresienstadt. That was the first one.

speaker: interviewee
text: We in Slovakia were terribly surprised and scared because we knew then we have-- we will have to follow. Then the Germans will not leave Slovakia as a independent state in the pocket, freedom to Jews, which unfortunately occurred, but not immediately. Up to the year '41, the anti-Jewish laws were introduced slowly.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go after you had to leave the textile school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had to go home.

speaker: interviewer
text: To Bratislava?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Bratislava. My parents had very big business, my father. So and-- I didn't-- I couldn't study. There were no textile academy in Slovakia at all. So I was just helping in the firm of my-- my father, which was a-- we had retail shops, and we had wholesale business. So we had a lot of people, about 27 people, employed in the several shops.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was just one of the youngest son. My older brother was, like, the managing director of the business, and my father was the-- the owner of the big business. It was a multimillion-dollar firm. So I-- I found occupation in the-- in the business of my family. But I was not-- you see, that was a different setup that time, not like now, when if you work even for your own father's firm, you are employed, and you are legally employed.

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't such a setup that time. I was just the son of the owner, has nothing to do. So I-- I was living in the-- in the house of our family, and I went daily to work in the shop. What sort of work I have done was various, wherever it was needed. I had to go maybe to banks and to-- to supervise. We had several retail shops. So one day I was there, the other. My father was very advanced businessman. He-- he-- for instance, I remember when he-- when he put me in the shop, one of the shops, he called the-- the manageress-- we had a woman manageress. He said, look, I-- put him in which department you want, and don't-- don't handle him like a son of-- of the owner. He is employed here. You are in charge. And I remember [LAUGHS] this lady who was in charge of the whole retail shop. And we had ladies' wear, and we had ladies' underwear. And she put me, directly, maybe, in the ladies' underwear section. And you can imagine when you are 16 and 1/2 or 17, we-- we were not like the youngsters of today. I couldn't understand. How can I-- I was not able to talk to a lady, especially about these things, so it was quite interesting.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We're going to change the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, that's the end of tape one of the interview with John Horak. It's the 18th of June, 1995, and we're in Sydney, Australia.
